items.title: End Semester Examination Scheme
items.content: Scheme of End Semester Examination/Assessment: Internal Assessment (20 marks) - Assignment (10 marks), Attendance (10 marks). Theory paper Exam (20 marks). Sport Specific Practical Assessment (60 marks) - Basic Skills demonstration, Match practice.

items.title: Athletics Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Athletics (Track & Field): Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Athletics; Structure & Function of World Athletics and AFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Running, Jumping, Throwing Techniques, Event-Specific Conditioning (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Track and Field Event Rules, Scoring, Timing, Measurement (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Meet Management, Event Scheduling, Heat Structures (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment, totaling 16 hours across 16 weeks.

items.title: Athletics Theory Chapters
items.content: Athletics Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Athletics - Origins in Ancient Greece and Olympic revival in 1896, Development of structured events by IAAF (now World Athletics), Role of Athletics in physical development and talent scouting in schools. 2. Governing Bodies - World Athletics (formerly IAAF): International body setting global rules and records, AFI (Athletics Federation of India): Governs all Indian athletics competitions, talent ID, and development. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Sprinting: Block starts, acceleration, stride frequency; Middle and Long Distance: Pacing strategy, breathing rhythm; Hurdles: Step pattern, hurdle clearance; Jumps: Long/Triple Jump: Approach run, take-off, flight, and landing; High Jump: Fosbury Flop technique; Pole Vault: Grip, pole plant, bar clearance; Throws: Shot Put, Discus, Javelin, Hammer: Grip, stance, rotation/glide, release mechanics. 4. Rules and Regulations - Track Rules: Lane violations, false start, relay exchange zones; Field Rules: Number of attempts, fouls, valid measurement zones; Equipment Regulations: Size, weight, and type of implements; Scoring System: Based on performance (distance/time), and position in heats/finals. 5. Officiating - Roles of Referee, Starter, Timekeeper, Measurer; Use of electronic timing systems; Measurement of throws/jumps using standard tools; Use of red and white flags for validity signals. 6. Event Organization - Heats & Finals: Time-based and position-based qualifications; Facility Setup: Track markings, field zones, event-specific safety; Scorekeeping & Results Compilation; Roles of announcer, volunteers, marshals, and technical officials.

items.title: Athletics Practical Sessions
items.content: Athletics Practical Sessions: A. Running Events - Sprint Drills: High knees, A/B skips, block starts; Middle-distance: Tempo runs, pace control drills; Relay practice: Baton hand-off in exchange zones. B. Jumping Events - Long jump & triple jump phases; High jump: Approach curve and takeoff; Pole vault drills (if facilities available). C. Throwing Events - Shot put, discus, javelin, and hammer drills; Use of light implements for technique; Safety zone briefing and warm-up protocol. D. Conditioning & Flexibility - Strength training circuits; Dynamic and static stretching; Mobility drills for jumpers and throwers.

items.title: Athletics Assessment
items.content: Athletics Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - History, rules of events, equipment standards, officiating signals. 2. Practical Exam - Event-specific technique execution; Participation in mock meet: sprints, jump, and throw; Teamwork, warm-up routine, adherence to rules.

items.title: Athletics References
items.content: Athletics References: 'Fundamentals of Track and Field' – Gerry Carr, 'Athletics: Steps to Success' – Ed Jacoby & Bob Fraley, IAAF (World Athletics) Coaching Manuals – Available at worldathletics.org

items.title: Badminton Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Badminton: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Badminton; Structure & Function of BWF and BAI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Grips, Strokes, Footwork, Movement, Singles and Doubles Tactics (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Scoring System, Match Format, Referee and Umpire Roles (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Tournament Format, Draw System, Scheduling and Scoring (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment, totaling 16 hours across 16 weeks.

items.title: Badminton Theory Chapters
items.content: Badminton Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Badminton - Originated in India as 'Poona'; developed in England; Establishment of BWF (1934), Olympic sport since 1992; India's rise in world badminton (P.V. Sindhu, Saina Nehwal, Srikanth Kidambi). 2. Governing Bodies - BWF (Badminton World Federation): Global rules, events like World Championships & Olympics; BAI (Badminton Association of India): Manages national ranking tournaments, coaching, selections. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Grips: Forehand grip, backhand grip; Footwork: Lunge, chasse, split-step, recovery; Strokes: Serve: Low serve, high serve; Lift: Underhand and overhand; Drop: Fast and slow; Smash: Jump and half-smash; Net Play: Tumble, net kill; Movement: Base position, return-to-base strategy. 4. Rules and Regulations - Scoring System: 21-point rally format, best of 3 games; Service Rules: Below waist height, service box regulations; Faults: Double hit, shuttle caught in net, foot faults; Match Setup: Singles and doubles court size and rotation rules. 5. Officiating - Umpire: Controls the match, keeps score; Service Judge: Observes service legality; Line Judges: Monitor boundary calls; Referee: Oversees the conduct of the event. 6. Event Organization - Tournament formats: Knockout, Round Robin; Seeding, draw creation, and scheduling; Match scheduling, scorer's table, warm-up time; Fair play principles and match protocols.

items.title: Badminton Practical Sessions
items.content: Badminton Practical Sessions: A. Skill Development - Serve, net play, lifts, and smashes; Grip corrections and racket control drills. B. Movement Training - Shuttle run drills; Multi-directional footwork; Corner recovery and court coverage patterns. C. Match Play Simulation - Singles rally practice; Doubles positioning and communication; Live scoring with match official roles practiced. D. Fitness and Conditioning - Agility ladder drills; Reaction time drills; Flexibility and cool-down routines.

items.title: Badminton Assessment
items.content: Badminton Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - History, rules, techniques, terminology, and scoring systems. 2. Practical Exam - Skill execution: Serve, smash, footwork; Match play under scoring conditions; Teamwork and adherence to game rules.

items.title: Badminton References
items.content: Badminton References: 'Fundamentals of Badminton' – Dr. Ramesh Chand, 'Techniques of Badminton' – Dr. Kiran Chhokar

items.title: Basketball Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Basketball: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Basketball; Structure & Function of FIBA and BFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Dribbling, Passing, Shooting, Rebounding, Defense, Team Play (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Game Rules, Scoring, Fouls, Referee Signals (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Tournament Format, Team Management, Scheduling (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment, totaling 16 hours across 16 weeks.

items.title: Basketball Theory Chapters
items.content: Basketball Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Basketball - Invented by Dr. James Naismith in 1891 (USA); Growth through YMCA and global expansion; Became an Olympic sport in 1936; Introduction and rise of basketball in India. 2. Governing Bodies - FIBA (International Basketball Federation): Governs global rules and tournaments; BFI (Basketball Federation of India): Governs basketball in India, conducts nationals and talent programs. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Dribbling: Low dribble, crossover, spin move; Passing: Accurate delivery under pressure; Shooting: Lay-ups, free throws, jump shots, three-pointers; Rebounding: Boxing out, timing, positioning; Defense: On-ball defense, help defense, stealing; Team Play: Transition offense, fast breaks, spacing. 4. Rules and Regulations - Team Composition: 5 players on court per team; Match Format: Four quarters of 10 minutes (FIBA); Scoring: 2-point, 3-point, and 1-point shots (free throws); Violations: Traveling, double dribble, shot clock, 3-second rule; Fouls: Personal, technical, unsportsmanlike fouls; Bonus Rule: Free throws after team fouls. 5. Officiating - Referee: Enforces rules, manages game flow; Umpires: Assist in foul calls, out-of-bound judgments; Scorekeeper & Timekeeper: Maintain game stats and timing; Signals: Use of hand signals for fouls, violations, and substitutions. 6. Event Organization - Tournament Types: Knockout, league, 3x3; Team Registration: Entry forms, player list, jersey numbers; Scheduling: Time slots, warm-up time, score recording; Logistics: Court preparation, first-aid, volunteer duties.

items.title: Basketball Practical Sessions
items.content: Basketball Practical Sessions: A. Skill Development - Dribbling under pressure; Passing in motion; Shooting drills from various positions. B. Defensive Drills - Close-outs, help defense, switching; Defensive slide and block out. C. Game Strategy Practice - Running offensive plays; 3v3 and 5v5 mock games; Time-bound scenarios (last 2 minutes of game). D. Fitness and Conditioning - Sprinting and shuttle runs; Agility ladder and cone drills; Vertical jump and core strength exercises.

items.title: Basketball Assessment
items.content: Basketball Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - History, rules, signals, scoring system, game structure. 2. Practical Exam - Skill demonstration (dribble, pass, shoot, defend); Participation in a mock game; Communication and adherence to team strategy.

items.title: Basketball References
items.content: Basketball References: 'Techniques and Tactics of Basketball' – Dr. Rajesh Kumar, 'Basketball Fundamentals' – Dr. P.C. Sharma

items.title: Cricket Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Cricket: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Cricket; Structure & Function of ICC and BCCI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Batting, Bowling, Fielding, Wicket-keeping, Running Between Wickets (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Match Formats, Scoring, Umpiring Signals (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Team Formation, Tournament Formats, Scoring Sheets (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment — a total of 16 hours across 16 weeks.

items.title: Cricket Theory Chapters
items.content: Cricket Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Cricket - Origins in England, expansion to colonies; Formation of ICC and its role; Evolution of formats: Test → ODI → T20. 2. Governing Bodies - ICC (International Cricket Council): Global governing body; BCCI (Board of Control for Cricket in India): National body for Indian cricket. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Batting: Defensive and attacking strokes, footwork; Bowling: Fast, swing, off-spin, leg-spin basics; Fielding: Catching (high, low), throwing accuracy, diving techniques; Wicket-Keeping: Foot movement, glove work, reflex catching; Running: Quick singles, turning, communication between batters. 4. Rules and Regulations - Match formats and structure; Field restrictions (Powerplays), Free Hits; LBW, Run out, Caught behind, DRS overview; Umpiring signals: Out, No-ball, Wide, Six, Four, Leg Bye. 5. Officiating - Role of field umpires and third umpire; Scoring system and how matches are recorded; Role of match referee and scorers. 6. Event Organization - Team registration, fixture planning; Scoring formats: Manual and Digital; Ground setup and logistics management.

items.title: Cricket Practical Sessions
items.content: Cricket Practical Sessions: A. Batting Drills - Front foot drives, pull shots, cut shots; Defense and shot selection under pressure. B. Bowling Drills - Target bowling, pace control, spin variations; Run-up and follow-through training. C. Fielding Drills - One-hand pick-up and throw; Slip catching, outfield high catches, direct hits. D. Game Simulation - T20 practice match; Rotating players through all roles (batting, bowling, fielding, keeping).

items.title: Cricket Assessment
items.content: Cricket Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - Written paper covering rules, history, equipment, and techniques. 2. Practical Exam - Demonstration of batting, bowling, and fielding techniques; Match-play performance and tactical application.

items.title: Cricket References
items.content: Cricket References: 'Cricket: Skills and Techniques' – Dr. Ramesh Rawat, 'Fundamentals of Cricket' – Dr. B.S. Bal

items.title: Football Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Football: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Football; Structure & Function of FIFA and AIFF (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Dribbling, Passing, Shooting, Ball Control, Defending, Goalkeeping (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Competition Formats, Scoring, and Refereeing (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Match Preparation, Team Strategy, Tournament Organization (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment — a total of 16 hours over 16 weeks.

items.title: Football Theory Chapters
items.content: Football Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Football - Origins of football, formation of FIFA; Evolution of rules and modern football (Club & International level). 2. Governing Bodies - FIFA (Fédération Internationale de Football Association): Global regulation; AIFF (All India Football Federation): National governing body. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Dribbling: Ball control, close control, change of pace; Passing: Short and long-range, through balls; Shooting: Techniques for accuracy, power, and different angles; Defending: Tackling, marking, and positioning; Goalkeeping: Shot-stopping, distribution, and communication. 4. Rules and Regulations - Match Formats: Duration of games, extra time, and penalty shootouts; Offside Rule: Definition, exceptions, and impact on strategy; Fouls & Cards: Yellow and red cards, free kicks, penalty kicks; Refereeing: Role of the referee, assistant referees, and video assistant referee (VAR). 5. Officiating - Referee's Role: Conducting matches, enforcing laws, timekeeping; Assistant Referees: Offside decisions, throw-ins, and corner kicks; VAR: Overview of the video assistant referee's role. 6. Event Organization - Team Management: Team selection, pre-match warm-up routines; Match Setup: Field preparation, goal settings, match schedules; Tournament Management: Group stages, knockout rounds, and finals.

items.title: Football Practical Sessions
items.content: Football Practical Sessions: A. Skill Drills - Passing, Dribbling, and Shooting drills; Ball control and receiving practice; Goalkeeping basics: Catching, diving, distribution. B. Defensive Drills - One-on-one defending, tackling, intercepting passes; Zonal defending, pressing high up the pitch. C. Team Play and Strategy - Possession-based play, counter-attacking drills; Positioning exercises, understanding formations (4-4-2, 4-3-3). D. Match Simulation - Small-sided games for speed and tactics; Full-field practice matches for team play, defensive and offensive transitions.

items.title: Football Assessment
items.content: Football Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - Written exam covering rules, history, techniques, and governing bodies. 2. Practical Exam - Skill demonstration (dribbling, passing, shooting); Defensive and attacking scenarios during match play.

items.title: Football References
items.content: Football References: 'Football Fundamentals and Techniques' – Dr. Rakesh Tomar, 'Football Coaching Manual' – SAI Publication

items.title: Judo Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Judo: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Judo; Structure & Function of IJF and JFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Ukemi (Breakfalls), Kuzushi, Nage-waza, Katame-waza, Randori (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Scoring System, Penalties, Referee Commands (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Tournament Format, Weight Categories, Safety Protocols (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment, totaling 16 hours spread across 16 weeks.

items.title: Judo Theory Chapters
items.content: Judo Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Judo - Founded by Jigoro Kano in 1882 in Japan; Development from traditional jujutsu; Olympic debut in 1964 (men) and 1992 (women); Role of Judo in character education and peace promotion. 2. Governing Bodies - IJF (International Judo Federation): Sets global rules and organizes World Championships, Olympics; JFI (Judo Federation of India): Manages national events and athlete development in India. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Ukemi (Breakfalls): To fall safely and prevent injury; Kuzushi: Unbalancing the opponent; Nage-waza (Throwing Techniques): Hand, hip, foot, and sacrifice throws; Katame-waza (Grappling Techniques): Pins (Osaekomi-waza), chokes (Shime-waza), joint locks (Kansetsu-waza). 4. Rules and Regulations - Scoring: Ippon (full point), Waza-ari (half), Shido (penalty); Prohibited Actions: Grabbing legs, defensive posture, illegal grips; Match Duration: 4 minutes for cadets/juniors/seniors (IJF standard); Victory Conditions: Ippon, accumulation of Waza-ari, opponent disqualification. 5. Officiating - Referee Commands: Hajime (start), Matte (stop), Ippon, Waza-ari, Shido; Judging Panel: Referee and 2 corner judges (video review in major events); Mat Layout: Contest area and safety zone. 6. Event Organization - Weight Classes: Based on age and gender categories; Competition Format: Knockout, pool rounds, and repechage; Draw System: Seeding, brackets, and medals; Role of Coaches and Officials: Conduct, mat-side behavior.

items.title: Judo Practical Sessions
items.content: Judo Practical Sessions: A. Basic Skills and Drills - Breakfalls and footwork; Repetitive throwing drills (uchi-komi); Movement drills with partner. B. Technique Execution - Practice of 2–3 throws per session; Pinning combinations and transitions; Shime-waza and Kansetsu-waza under supervision. C. Randori (Free Practice) - Standing (tachi-waza) and ground (ne-waza) sparring; Situational randori (start in pin, escape drills). D. Competition Simulation - Mini matches with rules; Practice referee commands and scoring; Respect and bowing rituals before and after match.

items.title: Judo Assessment
items.content: Judo Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - History, philosophy, rules, techniques, terminology. 2. Practical Exam - Execution of breakfalls, throws, and pins; Match participation and sportsmanship evaluation.

items.title: Judo References
items.content: Judo References: 'Fundamentals of Judo' – Dr. G.L. Khanna, 'Judo: A Way of Life' – Sports Authority of India (SAI Manual)

items.title: Squash Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Squash: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Squash; Structure & Function of WSF and SRFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Grip, Strokes, Movement, Court Awareness, Match Play (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Scoring System, Refereeing, Code of Conduct (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Tournament Structure, Draws, Match Scheduling (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment — a total of 16 hours over 16 weeks.

items.title: Squash Theory Chapters
items.content: Squash Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Squash - Origin from the game of racquets in England; Growth as a competitive international sport; Inclusion in major events (Commonwealth Games, Asian Games). 2. Governing Bodies - WSF (World Squash Federation): Global governing authority; SRFI (Squash Rackets Federation of India): National organization. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Grip: Standard racket hold; Forehand & Backhand: Drives, drops, volleys; Movement: T-position recovery, ghosting drills; Game Patterns: Length and width play, attacking front court. 4. Rules and Regulations - Game structure: Best of 3 or 5 games to 11 points; Scoring system: PAR (Point-A-Rally); Interference, let, stroke decisions; Code of conduct and safety guidelines. 5. Officiating - Role of Referee and Marker; Call commands: 'Let', 'Stroke', 'No Let'; Managing disputes and conduct violations. 6. Event Organization - Draw format: Knockout, Round Robin; Court setup and scheduling; Player briefing, warm-up zones, scoreboard handling.

items.title: Squash Practical Sessions
items.content: Squash Practical Sessions: A. Technique Development - Drive consistency and control; Basic drop shots and front court play; Volley timing and quick reaction. B. Footwork & Agility - Court movement drills; Ghosting drills for positioning and recovery; Front-back and side-to-side transitions. C. Match Play Preparation - Serve & return drills; Constructing rallies with intention; Short format matches with scoring. D. Physical Fitness & Injury Prevention - Core strength training; Dynamic stretching routines; Eye protection and safety awareness.

items.title: Squash Assessment
items.content: Squash Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - History, rules, structure of the game, and scoring system. 2. Practical Exam - Skill tests on grip, stroke execution, movement; Observation in mini matches for application and tactics.

items.title: Squash References
items.content: Squash References: 'Squash: Skills – Tactics – Techniques' – Tony Griffin, 'The Complete Guide to Coaching Squash' – Ian McKenzie

items.title: Swimming Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Swimming: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Swimming; Structure & Function of FINA and SFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Floating, Breathing, Freestyle, Backstroke, Breaststroke, Butterfly (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Stroke Rules, Start & Turn Techniques, Race Management (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Heats, Finals, Relays, Safety Measures (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment, totaling 16 hours across 16 weeks.

items.title: Swimming Theory Chapters
items.content: Swimming Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Swimming - Ancient records of swimming (Egypt, Greece); Competitive swimming began in the 1800s; Introduction to Olympics in 1896 (men) and 1912 (women); India's milestones and international achievements. 2. Governing Bodies - FINA (World Aquatics): Sets global rules, organizes World Championships, Olympics; SFI (Swimming Federation of India): Manages national events and athlete training. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Floating: Front and back float; Breathing: Side breathing and rhythmic coordination; Strokes: Freestyle: Flutter kick, arm pull, streamline position; Backstroke: Supine body position, alternating arm and kick; Breaststroke: Simultaneous kick and pull, glide technique; Butterfly: Dolphin kick, simultaneous arm pull. 4. Rules and Regulations - Starts: Dive technique, false start rules; Turns: Stroke-specific rules for legal turns; Finishes: One-hand vs two-hand touches; Stroke Violations: Incorrect movement, pull-out violations; Disqualification Scenarios: False start, early finish, illegal stroke. 5. Officiating - Referee: Supervises overall conduct; Starter: Ensures fair start, uses whistle and buzzer; Timekeepers: Record swimmers' finish times; Stroke Judges: Monitor legality of movements; Turn Judges: Ensure correct turn techniques. 6. Event Organization - Race Formats: 25m, 50m, 100m, 200m, IM, Relays; Heats and Finals: Time-based qualification; Relay Events: Team entries, changeovers; Safety: Lifeguards, first aid, pool regulations.

items.title: Swimming Practical Sessions
items.content: Swimming Practical Sessions: A. Skill Development - Floating and breathing control; Stroke technique drills using kickboards and paddles; Arm recovery and leg propulsion drills. B. Stroke Training - Freestyle drills with breathing focus; Backstroke with correct hand entry; Breaststroke timing and coordination; Butterfly kick and rhythm improvement. C. Competitive Readiness - Start practice with dive blocks; Turn practice at wall (open turn & flip turn); Relay practice (takeover timing). D. Conditioning & Endurance - Lap swimming for stamina; Interval training sets; Breath-holding drills.

items.title: Swimming Assessment
items.content: Swimming Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - Written paper on stroke rules, start/turn protocols, and history. 2. Practical Exam - Stroke demonstration (at least 2 strokes); Timing trial in a chosen event; Turn and finish technique evaluation.

items.title: Swimming References
items.content: Swimming References: 'Squash: Skills – Tactics – Techniques' – Tony Griffin, 'The Complete Guide to Coaching Squash' – Ian McKenzie

items.title: Table Tennis Curriculum
items.content: Table Tennis Curriculum: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Table Tennis; Structure & Function of ITTF and TTFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Grips and Strokes, Footwork and Rally Drills, Participation in Competition, Advanced Drills for Skill Enhancement (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Match Formats, Scoring, and Equipment (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Basic Event Management in Table Tennis (20%). Each semester will consist of 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment. The total 16 hours will be split into 16 weeks.

items.title: Table Tennis Theory Chapters
items.content: Table Tennis Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Table Tennis - Origins in 19th-century England; Evolution into an Olympic sport (1988); Rise of dominant nations (China, Sweden, Germany); Development in India: Role of TTFI. 2. Governing Bodies - ITTF (International Table Tennis Federation): Headquarters: Lausanne, Switzerland; Oversees world rankings, rules, events like World Championships, Olympics; TTFI (Table Tennis Federation of India): Founded in 1926; Organizes national championships, junior development, selection for international events. 3. Fundamental Skills and Drills - Grips: Shakehand, Penhold; Strokes: Forehand, Backhand, Push, Chop, Loop; Footwork: Side shuffle, crossover step; Drills: Multi-ball drills, Service-receive combinations, Consistency and speed drills. 4. Rules and Regulations - Match formats (Best of 5 or 7); 11-point games, switch every 2 serves; Legal service rules (16 cm toss, open palm); Singles vs Doubles rules; Equipment specs: Bat, ball (40 mm), table dimensions. 5. Officiating in Table Tennis - Role of umpire, assistant umpire, referee; Scoring, time-outs, and let rules; Use of video review (if applicable in tournaments). 6. Organization of Events - Setting up tables and courts; Round robin vs knockout formats; Draw creation, seeding, and scheduling; Score sheet preparation; Managing team events and singles fixtures.

items.title: Table Tennis Practical Sessions
items.content: Table Tennis Practical Sessions: A. Stroke Practice - Forehand and backhand drives; Push and block techniques; Loop and counter-loop; Smash and lob. B. Footwork Drills - Side-to-side movement; Short-long transitions; Position recovery. C. Service and Receive - Spin generation; Placement and deception; Third-ball attack setups. D. Match Play and Simulation - Singles and Doubles practice matches; Tactical decision-making; Adapting to opponent's style.

items.title: Table Tennis Assessment
items.content: Table Tennis Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - Written test on rules, history, techniques, and event structure. 2. Practical Exam - Execution of strokes, serves, footwork; Match-play performance and tactical application.

items.title: Table Tennis References
items.content: Table Tennis References: 'Table Tennis: Techniques and Rules' – Dr. Pankaj Singh, 'Fundamentals of Table Tennis' – NCERT/CBSE PE curriculum

items.title: Taekwondo Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Taekwondo: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Taekwondo; Structure & Function of WT and TFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Stances, Kicks, Punches, Blocks, Poomsae, Sparring (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Scoring System, Competition Format, Refereeing (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Belt Testing, Tournament Management, Safety (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment — a total of 16 hours over 16 weeks.

items.title: Taekwondo Theory Chapters
items.content: Taekwondo Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Taekwondo - Origins in Korea, meaning of 'Tae-Kwon-Do'; Evolution into Olympic sport; Role of martial arts in discipline and character-building. 2. Governing Bodies - WT (World Taekwondo): Governs Olympic-style sparring; TFI (Taekwondo Federation of India): National authority in India. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Stances: Attention, Ready, Front, Back; Kicks: Front Kick, Turning Kick, Back Kick, Jump Kick; Hand Techniques: Jab, Cross, Palm Strike; Blocks: Low, Middle, High, Knife-Hand; Poomsae: Forms or patterns for belt advancement; Sparring (Kyorugi): Controlled combat techniques. 4. Rules and Regulations - Olympic sparring rules; Points system: Head kick, body kick, punches; Protective gear: Headguard, chest protector, gloves; Warnings, penalties, disqualification rules. 5. Officiating - Referee's Role: Starting match, scoring calls, issuing penalties; Judges: Point assessment through electronic scoring; Use of Instant Video Replay (IVR).

items.title: Taekwondo References
items.content: Taekwondo References: 'Taekwondo: Basic Techniques' – Dr. Harish Chander, 'Taekwondo: The State of the Art' – Master James S. Benko

items.title: Volleyball Curriculum
items.content: Curriculum of Volleyball: Unit 1 - Origin and Latest Development of Volleyball; Structure & Function of FIVB and VFI (20%). Unit 2 - Fundamental Skills Training: Serving, Passing, Setting, Spiking, Blocking, Team Formation (40%). Unit 3 - Rules, Regulations and Officiating: Game Format, Scoring, Refereeing System (20%). Unit 4 - Event Organization and Participation: Match Preparation, Tournament Conduct, Roles of Officials (20%). Each semester includes 4 hours of theory, 10 hours of practicals, and 2 hours of assessment, totaling 16 hours spread across 16 weeks.

items.title: Volleyball Theory Chapters
items.content: Volleyball Theory Chapters: 1. History and Development of Volleyball - Invented by William G. Morgan in 1895 (USA); Spread globally under YMCA; recognition by IOC; Olympic status and introduction of variations (Beach Volleyball, Sitting Volleyball). 2. Governing Bodies - FIVB (Fédération Internationale de Volleyball): World governing body; VFI (Volleyball Federation of India): Organizes and regulates volleyball in India. 3. Fundamental Techniques - Serve: Underhand, Overhand, Jump Serve; Pass: Forearm pass (bump), overhead pass (set); Set: Front set, back set, quick set; Spike: Approach, jump, arm swing; Block: Solo and double block techniques; Dig: Defensive skill for receiving spikes. 4. Rules and Regulations - Team Composition: 6 players per side; Match Format: Best of 3 or 5 sets, sets to 25 points; Rotation and Substitution Rules; Net Violations, Foot Faults, Double Hits; Line Calls and Referee Signals. 5. Officiating - First Referee: Controls the match from referee stand; Second Referee: Assists near the net, monitors rotation; Line Judges: Flag calls on court boundaries; Scorer: Maintains official score sheet and substitutions. 6. Event Organization - Setup of court and net; Scheduling matches, team registration; Roles of volunteers, coaches, and referees; Conducting inter-house or inter-school tournaments.

items.title: Volleyball Practical Sessions
items.content: Volleyball Practical Sessions: A. Skill Drills - Passing: Wall drills, partner drills; Serving: Toss and serve accuracy; Setting: Height and direction control; Spiking: Approach, jump, and hitting point. B. Team Drills - 3-player passing system; Rotation practice and position-specific roles; Simulated rallies for attack-defense transition. C. Game Simulation - Mini games focusing on rule application; Serving under pressure and tactical placement; Using real match situations to evaluate decision-making. D. Physical Conditioning - Vertical jump training; Core and shoulder strength workouts; Agility and movement around court.

items.title: Volleyball Assessment
items.content: Volleyball Assessment: 1. Theory Exam - Written test on rules, history, court layout, and scoring. 2. Practical Exam - Execution of basic skills: Serve, Pass, Set, Spike, Block; Team coordination and participation in a practice match.

items.title: Volleyball References
items.content: Volleyball References: 'Volleyball: Fundamental Skills' – Dr. Ajay Kumar, 'Coaching Volleyball' – LNIPE Publication

items.title: Campus Prayer
items.content: Campus Prayer (Sanskrit): OM. Whole is that (Brahman), whole also is this (world). From whole comes the whole; take whole from whole; (yet) whole remains. OM. Whatever exists in this world is to be enveloped by God (The Ruler). By renouncing it (the world) thou mayst enjoy. Do not covet the riches of anyone. I bow down to Madhava, the Bliss Eternal; to Him whose grace enables the dumb to speak (and) the lame to go across a mountain. OM. May Sarasvati, the Divine protect us by her strength-giving favours, she who is rich in strength-giving favours, the projector of our thoughts. Sarasvati, who inspires hymns and promotes pious thoughts, brings about our sacrifice. OM. From the unreal lead me to the real. From darkness leads me to light. From death lead me to immortality. OM. May God protect us both at the same time; at the same time support us both. May both of us at the same time apply (our) strength. May our learning be illustrious. May there be no hatred (between us). May all here be happy. May all be free from disease. May all see well-being. May nobody experience misery. OM. Peace, Peace, Peace.

items.title: Somaiya Vidyavihar University Overview
items.content: Somaiya Vidyavihar University (SVU) is an educational institution of academic brilliance, embodying over 80 years of educational leadership and innovation. Established as a private university by the Somaiya Vidyavihar University, Mumbai Act of 2019, it thrives on a sprawling, green, 50-acre campus in the heart of Mumbai. Here, more than 10,000 students immerse themselves in a wide spectrum of disciplines—from Management, Engineering, and Design to Humanities and the Sciences. With its foundation laid by the visionary Padmabhushan K. J. Somaiya, the University offers a transformative educational experience where students enjoy the intellectual freedom to explore diverse 'major-minor' combinations. This distinctive approach encourages academic exploration and lifelong connections with a global alum network that extends far beyond the campus. Recognised under the UGC Act 1956, SVU opens doors to global opportunities. Students benefit from an advanced incubation centre that nurtures budding entrepreneurs, robust placement services that bridge the gap between education and industry, and world-class on-campus accommodation. The guiding ethos of 'Freedom of Possibilities' empowers students to create their path to excellence, growth, and self-discovery. With an ambition to craft an institution that is both unapologetically Indian and globally renowned for research, Somaiya Vidyavihar stands tall. Its 40 affiliate institutions educate over 39,000 students, supported by a distinguished faculty of 1,500, spread across its two main campuses at Vidyavihar and Ayurvihar in Sion, Mumbai. Additionally, smaller campuses across rural Maharashtra, Karnataka, Gujarat, and Madhya Pradesh extend the reach of its academic prowess. These educational institutions offer various undergraduate, postgraduate, and doctoral programmes, along with vocational training and schools that instruct in English, Gujarati, and Marathi from pre-primary to higher secondary. At SVU, education is not merely imparted but lived. It is an ecosystem where knowledge is preserved, shared, and created anew—fuelled by disciplined commitment to research, consultancy, and service. The University is celebrated not only for its excellence in Science, Technology, Engineering, Medicine, Management, Social Sciences, and the Humanities but also for its unique emphasis on studying the diverse faiths and cultures of India. SVU is a sanctuary for innovation, intellectual curiosity, and leadership, inspiring students to transcend the ordinary and shape a future without limits.

items.title: K J Somaiya Institute of Management Overview
items.content: The K J Somaiya Institute of Management (KJSIM) in Mumbai stands as a distinguished business school, proudly affiliated with the esteemed Somaiya Vidyavihar University. Its inception dates to 1981, and over the years, it has risen to the ranks of the top 10 private business schools in India. KJSIM believes in enabling transformation journeys of individuals to value-based & purpose-driven leaders for building a better world. Nestled in a beautifully landscaped campus in the heart of the commercial capital of India, KJSIM acts as a perfect crucible for transformation of students. KJSIM offers core MBA, domain specific and multi-disciplinary programmes like MBA in Healthcare Management and MBA in Sports Management, Executive MBA, an MBA for working executives (online) and MCA programme in step with the requirements of the various sectors of the industry and growth opportunities in the economy. Apart from the regular programmes, the Institute offers customised and industry specific certificate and executive development programmes for government bodies, companies, defense personnel and NGOs. KJSIM also holds the coveted AACSB accreditation and has been recognised in India's 'Top 25 Business Schools'. With a razor-sharp goal of creating 'day-one ready graduates', the cornerstone of KJSIM's strength lies in the extensive industry collaborations and 15000+ global alumni base. It offers students opportunities such as guest speaker sessions, summer internships, capstone projects and mentorship from industry experts that align with tomorrow's industry requirements. With 20+ collaborations with international universities, exchange and immersion opportunities are available for students and faculty. KJSIM is led by Director & Dean, Raman Ramachandran, Ph.D, former Chairman and Managing Director of BASF India, is a committed proponent of bridging the gap between industry expectations and graduate outcomes. He spearheads a team of seasoned faculty and dedicated staff members.

items.title: Vision, Mission, Values and Purpose
items.content: Vision: Our dream is to build a world-class research and teaching institution that is global in the reach of its ideas and universal in its service. Mission: To foster a spirit of inquiry, enable livelihoods, encourage innovations and create good citizenships. Values: Excellence: Striving for higher standards in all endeavours. Integrity: Upholding honesty and strong moral principles. Collaborative: Fostering teamwork and shared success. Curiosity: Encouraging continuous learning and exploration. Humility: Valuing modesty and recognizing the contributions of others. Purpose: Transforming, Together. Together, we enable transformation journeys of individuals to value-based and purpose-driven leaders for building a better world.

items.title: Vice-Chancellor's Message
items.content: Dear Students, It is with great pleasure that I extend a warm welcome to each one of you, as you embark on a transformative journey at our K J Somaiya Institute of Management (KJSIM). On behalf of the faculty, staff, and the entire Somaiya Vidyavihar University community, I congratulate you on your acceptance into our MBA programme. At KJSIM, we have built a legacy of excellence in business education, providing students with the knowledge, skills, and experiences necessary to thrive in a rapidly changing global business landscape. Our MBA programme is designed to challenge you intellectually, broaden your perspectives, and equip you with the tools to become effective leaders and change agents in the world of business. As you commence your MBA studies, I encourage you to engage in rigorous academic pursuits, collaborate with diverse peers, and learn from our renowned faculty who bring a wealth of expertise and industry experience to the classroom. At KJSIM, you can take full advantage of the resources and support available to you. Our dedicated career services team is here to assist you in shaping your career path, connecting you with internships and job opportunities, and offering guidance on professional development. Engage in extracurricular activities, join student clubs, and participate in various events and conferences in the University campus. Our faculty and staff are here to support you on every step of the way. As you commence this transformative chapter of your academic and professional lives, I am confident that you will emerge as leaders who will shape the future of business. I wish you all the best on this exciting journey and encourage you to make the most of every opportunity that comes your way.

items.title: Director's Message
items.content: Dear Students, It is my privilege to welcome you all to this prestigious institute with a legacy of more than 40 years of quality business education. As you start this new phase of your life in our institute, I want to acknowledge and share the unprecedented legacy of our founder, Padma Bhushan Shri Karamshi Jethabhai Somaiya. Today, we are here because of his vision and commitment to the philosophy: 'ज्ञानादेव हि तत्त्वदर्शनम्' which means 'Knowledge Alone Liberates'. Karamshiji was an exceptional entrepreneur with exceptional grit and determination. Hailing from Kutch region, he moved to Mumbai in the pre-independence days and tried his hands at different business ventures. He was a Gandhian who preferred wearing khadi and conducted his business with impeccable values. Most significantly, he was a philanthropist who believed that the wealth you create must be used for the betterment of the country and society. Thus, he started his contribution to education by opening a school 82 years ago. So, as you study here over the next two years, I will urge you to remember the glorious legacy of this institution, imbibe and live by the principles he embodied. As you begin, it's natural to feel both excited and a bit apprehensive about what lies ahead. I encourage you to make the most of the next two years by fully engaging with our exceptional academic programme, seizing the opportunity to interact with industry leaders in the commercial capital of India, and utilising the sports facility to build physical fitness and mental resilience. Most importantly, take this time to cultivate a vast network of fellow students, alumni and faculty mentors who will support and guide you. I hope this handbook will be a useful reference during your stint here. I wish you all the very best and look forward to growing together!

items.title: General Rules and Regulations
items.content: KJSIM upholds a strong framework of rules and regulations designed to foster academic excellence, personal integrity, and a respectful campus environment. Students are expected to adhere to high standards of honesty, maintain regular attendance, dress appropriately, and use technology responsibly. From academic conduct to campus dining and communication, these guidelines ensure a disciplined and inclusive atmosphere conducive to learning and professional growth. 1. Academic Integrity: Honesty: All students are expected to maintain the highest standards of academic honesty. Cheating, plagiarism, or any form of academic dishonesty is strictly prohibited and will result in disciplinary action. Examinations: No unauthorised materials or communication are permitted during examinations. Any attempt to cheat will lead to strict disciplinary measures. Assignments: All submitted work must be original and properly referenced. In group assignments, the contributions of each member must be clearly reflected. 2. Attendance and Participation: Class Attendance: Regular attendance is mandatory. Students are expected to attend all classes, participate actively, and be punctual. Leave of Absence: For medical absences, a valid medical certificate must be provided. Participation: Active participation in class discussions, projects, and other activities is highly encouraged, and in many cases, it contributes to the final grade. 3. Dress Code: Professional Attire: Students are expected to dress in professional attire for classes, presentations, and events unless otherwise specified. Casual Attire: Casual dress is acceptable outside of formal events but should remain appropriate at all times. 4. Campus Dining Facilities: Vegetarian Menu: All meals served within campus dining facilities, including the cafeteria and canteen, are strictly vegetarian. Prohibited Items: Non-vegetarian food items, including eggs, meat, fish, and poultry, are prohibited within campus dining premises. 5. Use of Technology: Laptops and Devices: Laptops and other electronic devices may be in class solely for academic purposes. The use of social media or other non- academic activities during class is not allowed. Social Media: Students are expected to exercise responsibility when using social media, as their behaviour reflects on themselves and the Institute. Email Communication: Students are required to regularly check their university email for official communications and respond promptly. Prohibited and Inappropriate Websites: Accessing inappropriate or blocked websites related to gambling, hacking, hate speech, pornography, illegal activities, or any content that violates ethical, legal, or institutional guidelines is strictly prohibited. 7. Placement Assistance: Utilisation: Students are encouraged to make full use of placement assistance services for internships, job placements, and career guidance. Professionalism: When representing the Institute in any professional setting, students must adhere to the highest standards of professionalism. Adherence to Placement Policy: Non-adherence to the placement policy will lead to disciplinary action, leading to debarment from internships and final placements. 8. Compliance: Handbook Adherence: Students are required to abide by all rules and regulations set forth in this handbook. Failure to comply may lead to disciplinary action. Updates: Students are responsible for staying informed about any changes or updates to these rules and regulations. Declarations: Students are required to ensure the accuracy of all information provided in any declarations made during their time at the institute. Any false or incorrect declarations may result in termination from the programme.

items.title: KJSIM Code Of Conduct For Students
items.content: KJSIM upholds the values and principles instilled by our founder. In order to preserve the integrity of the campus, all students are expected to adhere to the following guidelines: 1. Anti-Ragging Policy: KJSIM enforces a Zero Tolerance policy towards any form of ragging. Ragging is strictly prohibited under Indian law, specifically the UGC Act of 1956 and the Indian Penal Code (IPC). The aim of this policy is to eliminate, prevent, and prohibit any instances of ragging on campus or within hostel premises. Should any such incident occur, students are required to report it immediately to the concerned authorities. 2. Prevention of Sexual Harassment: KJSIM maintains a Zero Tolerance policy towards sexual harassment in any form and is committed to creating a safe and healthy environment that is free from prejudice, gender bias, and harassment. Every individual at KJSIM, including students, faculty, and staff, has the right to be treated with dignity and respect. Sexual harassment is a serious offence, punishable by law, and will be dealt with sternly by the Institute. 3. Student Conduct and Responsibility: Students residing on campus are expected to conduct themselves with maturity, responsibility, and accountability throughout their stay. It is also essential that students uphold academic integrity. 4. Identification and Security: Students must carry their identity cards at all times while on campus. Security personnel, as well as faculty and administrative staff, may request to see your ID at any time. Failure to present your ID may result in disciplinary action. 5. Vehicle Policy: Bringing vehicles into the campus without prior permission is strictly prohibited. Students are advised to seek approval from the administration before bringing vehicles to the campus. 6 Respect for Property: Students are expected to respect and maintain the property of the Institute and the campus at all times. Any damage caused to campus facilities will result in disciplinary measures. It is also essential that students uphold a high standard of personal conduct on campus. 7. Prohibition of Sales: Students are not permitted to engage in the sale of goods on campus. Any items brought with the intention to sell will be confiscated by the Institute. 8.Substance Abuse: The possession or use of alcohol, drugs, tobacco, or any illegal substances is strictly prohibited on campus. 9. Commitment to Ethical Standards: Every member of the KJSIM community is expected to uphold the ethical standards of the Institute, ensuring that our reputation and values are maintained. As part of a larger academic and social community, each individual bears the responsibility to act with integrity and respect.

items.title: Parivartan - Enabling Transformation Journeys
items.content: KJSIM has put in place an integrated dynamic framework, 'Parivartan', to enable students' transformation during their 2-year MBA journey. It has two key pillars: Nurturing Potential (NP) and the Student Progression Index (SPI). 1. Nurturing Potential: Nurturing Potential helps students recognise their innate capabilities or competencies and take responsibility for their development. Based on research, KJSIM has identified seven competencies (listed below) that are critical to succeed in any function, along with differentiating competencies required to succeed in specific roles in the professional world of work: 1. Working with People (Collaboration) 2. Adhering to Principles and Values 3. Presenting and Communicating Information 4. Analysing 5. Learning and Researching 6. Delivering Results and Meeting Customer Expectations 7. Adapting and Responding to Change The students are assessed on these competencies using a psychometric test in association with a consulting firm. The students receive a self-evaluation report which highlights their relative strengths and development areas. The designated mentors for the students will receive the SHL report of their mentees and facilitate the students' development over the two years of their MBA journey through classroom sessions and various other initiatives. 2. Student Progression Index (SPI): KJSIM believes that true transformation happens through academic efforts within classrooms as well as experiences beyond the classroom. Student Progression Index is designed to encourage student participation in areas which enable their holistic development. The parameters for measuring student progression index are: 1. Academics (CGPA) – Students' academic performance (CGPA) constitutes 50% weightage. The CGPA gets averaged at every progressive semester. 2. Deep Reflective Learning through Ken & Economist (DRLK&E) – To inculcate the habit of reading and reflection, selected articles from The Ken and The Economist are shared with students on a weekly basis, along with possible reflective questions. Students are evaluated by their designated mentors, and it constitutes 15% weightage of which 7.5% for attending and 7.5% for worthy contributions in the broad discussion. Students are expected to participate in 5 discussions in each of the semesters in the first year. 3. Careers & Conversations (C&C) - Eminent speakers from different walks of life share their personal and professional voyages. This platform aims to provide ample learning opportunities through real-life stories. C&C constitutes 5% weightage for attending 3 sessions in 2 years. 4. Certifications – There is considerable evidence that industry-led certifications increase the probability of being hired with a higher salary. KJSIM encourages students to discuss with their mentors and enrol for industry-led certifications or pursue relevant certification to bridge the gap between academic theory and practical industry skills. one industry-led certification or three skill-based courses from Coursera, Udemy, edX or a similar learning platform over a period of 2 years (1 per semester), will constitute 10% weightage. 5. Competitions - KJSIM drives students to participate in case studies, business plans and sports competitions to complement academic learning and enhance their holistic development. Participation in two such competitions or winning a competition will constitute 10% weightage. 6. Health & Fitness - Maintaining good health and fitness levels can significantly enhance academic performance, stress management, and overall quality of life. Students enrol on fitness and sports sessions to keep themselves healthy and fit. Health & Fitness constitutes 10% weightage, of which 7% is for participation in any sport and 3% for performance evaluation. This will be calculated every semester. This framework underscores the following objectives for students: • Helps students gain self-awareness about their strengths and areas of development • Helps students to get an additional lens to make judicious choices relating to specialisation and career. • Instil habits and behaviours that will help to continuously adapt, evolve and thrive in the dynamic workplace. Parivartan Portal will be accessible to students through their Somaiya mail ID. This portal will enable students to access their fitment scores, set development goals through Parivartan action planner, choose components of SPI that will help them to fulfil those goals, interact with their mentors and be in the know of their progression score. At the end of every semester (for 3 semesters), the top 25 SPI scorers are duly rewarded by the institute.

items.title: Programmes Offered and Course Structure
items.content: KJSIM offers the following programmes: • Doctoral Programme (Ph.D) in Management • MBA • MBA Healthcare Management • MBA Sports Management • MBA Executive • Masters in Computer Applications. Masters of Business Administration: Semester I includes courses like Business Statistics, Cost Management and Control, Design Thinking and Entrepreneurship, Financial Reporting and Analysis, Marketing Management I, Microeconomics, Operations Management, Organizational Behaviour, Spreadsheet Modelling, Decision Science. Mandatory Audit Courses include Business Communication, Business Sustainability and Business Ethics, Health and Fitness. Semester II includes courses like Business Research Methods, Data Science Using Python, Financial Management, Human Resource Management, Logistics and Supply Chain Management, Macroeconomics, Marketing Management II, Project Management, Strategic Management, and one Elective. Mandatory Audit Courses include Business Communication, Business Sustainability and Business Ethics, Health and Fitness, Leadership Development. Master of Business Administration-Healthcare Management: Semester I includes courses like Business Communication & Report Writing, Epidemiology and Patient Care, Fundamentals of Finance, Fundamentals of Health Economics, Fundamentals of Marketing, Healthcare Marketing, Hospital Administration, Organizational Behaviour, Spreadsheet for Data Analytics, Statistical Analysis. Semester II includes courses like Cost & Managerial Accounting, Design Thinking and Entrepreneurship Management, Digital Marketing in Healthcare, Healthcare Services Management, Human Resource Management, Macroeconomic Aspects of Health Sector, Pharma and Biotech Management, Research Methodology, Operations and Supply Chain Management, and one Elective. Semester III includes courses like Advanced Analytics in Healthcare Using R & Python, Financial Management, International Business and Medical Tourism, Medico-Legal Environment and Ethics in Healthcare, Quality Management in Healthcare and Six Sigma, Retail Management in Healthcare. Summer Internship Project is also included. Semester IV includes courses like Customer Relationship Management and Integrated Marketing Communication, Emerging Technologies and New Product Development in Healthcare, Health Insurance & Financing, Healthcare in Rural Markets and NGO Management, Preventive Healthcare and Demographic Science, Strategic Brand Management. Capstone Project is also included. MBA-Sports Management (MBA-SM): Semester I includes courses like Business Communication, Human Behaviour and HR Practices in Sports Management, Introduction to Sports and Sports Industry, Management Accounting in Sports, Statistics in Sports Management, Advanced Excel, Sports Economics, Sports Marketing and Sponsorship Management, Sports Structure, Development and Governance, Sports Ventures, Innovation and CSR. Semester II includes courses like Applied Sports Marketing Research, Financial Management in Sports, Sports Analytics Management, Sports Events and Facilities Management, Sports Project Management, Technology in Sports, Legal and Ethics in Sports Management, Sports Operations & Supply Chain Management, Strategic Management. Winter Internship I is also included.

items.title: Major - Minor Policy
items.content: KJSIM-SVU/ Selection of Major-Minor – Batch 2025-27/Version 1.0/2025-26. Year 1: Foundational Learning and Industry Exposure. In the first year, students are introduced to all core functional areas of management through a comprehensive set of foundational courses. By the middle of the year, students will have developed an understanding of various disciplines within management and gained an overview of the industry. This is achieved through classroom learning as well as co-curricular engagements such as panel discussions, guest lectures, competitions, and capstone projects. Year 2: Specialisation through Majors and Minors. Major Specialisation: In the second year, students have the opportunity to specialise by selecting a major in specific management areas such as Business Analytics & Data Science, Finance, Human Resources (People Development), Marketing, and Operations. The major builds on the foundational knowledge acquired in the first year, offering ten specialised courses spread across the third and fourth semesters. Semester 3 includes six courses, while Semester 4 comprises four courses, collectively totalling to 30 credits (1,000 marks). A minimum of 20 students is required for a major to be offered. Objective of Major: The major enables students to develop in-depth expertise in their chosen area of specialisation. Through focused study, students build the competence necessary to excel in their field and prepare for industry-specific roles. Outcomes of Choosing a Major: Selecting a major equips students with specialised knowledge and skills, enhancing their readiness for the corporate world. It allows for a deeper understanding of the functional area, ultimately influencing students' short-term career decisions and improving their employability. The following five majors are offered to the students: 1. Business Analytics & Data Science 2. Finance 3. Human Resources (People Development) 4. Marketing 5. Operations. Minor Specialisation: Alongside the major, students also select a minor that complements their primary area of study. Objective of Choosing a Minor: The objective of the minor is to complement the student's selected major by offering additional knowledge in a related or entirely new area of management. It aims to provide a broader understanding of management disciplines beyond the student's primary specialisation. In doing so, the minor enables students to gain both practical and conceptual insights into an additional domain, thereby enhancing their interdisciplinary competence. Outcomes of Choosing a Minor: The minor enhances a student's overall academic and professional profile by equipping them with expertise in a secondary area of management. This additional specialisation broadens their perspective, fosters versatility, and prepares them for roles that demand cross-functional knowledge. By engaging with minor courses, students become more adaptable in dynamic work environments and are better positioned to pursue a wider range of career opportunities, both within and beyond their primary area of focus. The minor typically consists of four full-credit courses, evenly distributed across Semesters 3 and 4. However, in the case of Human Resources (People Development), the minor includes seven half-credit courses. Each minor course carries 100 marks, totalling 400 marks (12 credits) over the two semesters. A minimum of 20 students is required to offer any minor. Minors are offered in the following domains: 1. Business Analytics & Data Science 2. Business Economics 3. Finance 4. Human Resources (People Development) 5. Marketing 6. Operations 7. Strategy and Consulting. Students Can Choose the Major and Minor Based on: 1. Work experience 2. Competency 3. Interest, Inclination & Passion 4. External environment. Note: • Students are not permitted to select the same area of specialisation for both their Major and Minor. • Once selected, requests for changes to the Major or Minor will not be entertained. • Students will be assigned to divisions through a randomised process. As a result, they may not remain in the same division as in the first year, and division groupings may differ in the second year. Requests for changes in division assignments will not be entertained. • The Institute will allocate the Major and Minor specialisations to students who fail to submit their preferences within the stipulated timeline.

items.title: Attendance Policy
items.content: KJSIM-SVU/ Attendance Policy/Version 1.0/2025-2026. Revised on 31st March, 2025; valid for all programs started in academic year 2024-25 & 2025-26). At K J Somaiya Institute of Management (KJSIM), full attendance to the classes and coming on time is of utmost importance. This policy governs how attendance defaulters and late-comers are dealt with. Attendance carries 10 marks per course and will be a part of the Internal Assessment of the course. Internal assessment marks are 50 in case of full credit courses- bifurcated as follows: 40 marks for all other components and 10 marks for attendance. Marks will be deducted as below - per course attendance: • If the percentage of sessions attended is in decimal form, then it will be rounded off. • If the number of sessions in a course is only 15, then the minimum number of sessions to be attended is 11. • Exceptions: Medical reasons requiring hospitalisation or confinement, representing the institution in academic and sports competitions requiring students to be absent from the campus, or any other impactful activity for the institute, or exceptional situations where students had to attend placement-related activities. Absence due to placement-related activity should be approved by the Placement Department. • For medical reasons, a valid doctor's certificate and hospital discharge summary are required. For all others, a pre-approval by the process owner, who shall be informed immediately via email by the student or family member. These documents must be submitted to the programme office within three working days after joining. Late submissions shall not be entertained. Please note: Submission of fake medical certificates will result in disciplinary action. If a student fails to meet the attendance criteria repeatedly (attendance defaulter in multiple courses or attendance defaulter across semesters), the institute reserves the right to take punitive action, which may include not being allowed to sit for the exams. Attendance Marks allotted: 90-100%: 10, 80-89%: 8, 70%-79%: 6, <70%: 0. • Students' attendance records will be available on the MyAccount portal. In addition, on a rolling basis, defaulters will be informed that they do not meet the minimum 70% attendance and are likely to lose 10 marks in the internal assessment. A copy of such email may be sent to the student's parents/guardians. Students are expected to be in the classroom before a lecture is scheduled to start. Coming late to a class disrupts the flow of the class, both from the faculty's and fellow students' perspective. Students who are late by more than 15 minutes shall not be allowed to attend the class and shall be marked absent. For other latecomers (students late for a class less than 15 minutes), faculty may choose to: a) Grant attendance at their discretion b) Not allow the student into the class c) Mark the student absent, but allow the student to sit in the class d) Allow students who procure the 'Attendance Exemption Note' as mentioned below. A student is to come back with 'Attendance Exemption Note' duly signed and approved by the Director's Nominee. The forms will be available at the Admin office. (This can be done a maximum of two times in a particular course). Only the Programme Officers are authorised to make changes, if any, to student attendance records based on authorised exemptions (Placement, Admissions, Sports, and other teams shall provide exemption data to the PO team). The faculty is responsible for marking the attendance during the session only. The Programme Office will send the final attendance data sheet (after making changes as per the attendance policy) to the faculty concerned, who will include the marks in their internal mark sheet and send it to the exam department.
